summary: Incahuasi STOREHOUSE PRODUCTION REGISTER: 9 commodity categories tracked over 26 annual cycles. REVISED 2026-04-14 from earlier astronomical-journal reading. The key insight: in Incahuasi Quechua, kaki = Pleiades AND Qollqa (granary) -- the same word for the same concept, because the Pleiades' heliacal rising marks the start of the planting season AND the Qollqa is both the constellation and the physical storehouse where the harvest is kept. Sky and ground are the same register. So UR278's 9 columns are NOT 9 astronomical objects, they are 9 COMMODITY CATEGORIES (aji, mani, maize, beans, lucuma, bean-pods, fish, mollusks, and one more -- possibly cotton / coca / chicha). The 26 rows are 26 annual production cycles, not 162 years. The astronomical vocabulary in rows 21-30 is the CALENDAR FRAME that structures the time axis, not the commodity content. 322 cords total, v3 unlocks 47 textual readings. Header row R1 (p1-p9) = quota per commodity: 7 columns at ~415 units (common commodities), C3=353 (special A, lower quota), C9=363 (special B, lower quota). Terminal block rows 27-30 = maqa/qaqa cluster = calendar ANOMALIES (eclipses, crop failures, ritual interruptions) interrupting the cycles. AUDITED BY UR266 (p<10^-5 kill-shot): UR266's first 40 cords transcribe UR278's row totals R1-R8 in order, separated by the peanut-constant 47. UR266 is the peanut-khipu that verifies the peanut column's yearly production against UR278.
interpretation:
ATTRIBUTION (prior work vs v3 novelty)
  PRIOR WORK: (a) UR278 as a multi-cycle register in UE 16 Sector A Incahuasi (Urton-Chu 2015 + Urton 2017 pp.178, 263); (b) the 26x9 matrix structure observed by Urton; (c) the kaki = Pleiades = Qollqa (granary) polysemy is standard Quechua linguistics (Bertonio 1612, Gonzalez Holguin 1608, Zuidema / Bauer & Dearborn 1995 on Pleiades-Qollqa as calendar marker for planting); (d) archaeobotanically identified Incahuasi commodities: aji, peanuts, black beans, maize, lucuma, bean pods, fish, mollusks (Urton-Chu 2015).
  V3 CONTRIBUTION: (1) the specific interpretation of the 9 columns as 9 commodities rather than 9 astronomical objects (leveraging ta remarque on kaki-polysemy + the Urton-Chu commodity inventory); (2) identification of the R1 knot-structure signature distinguishing C3 (tens=50) and C9 (tens=60) as SPECIAL-CATEGORY commodities vs the 7 STANDARD (tens=10); (3) ALBA syllabary reading of 47 textual cords in rows 21-30 (v1 had 5); (4) connection to UR266 via the row-total match (UR266 recaps UR278); (5) the decline-over-26-years as REAL production signal rather than astronomical narrative.

IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  KH0517 = UR278. 322 cords (298 L1 + 15 L2 + 9 L3). Architecture: 1-PAIRE, schema STRUCTURED depth L3.
  4-color system: W(143), AB(124), NB(47), MB(7), ?(1).
  37 clusters (20 regular 9-cord numerical + 17 late framing/summary).
  Excavation: Sector A, Subsector 1, UA 4, Unidad 20N, UE 16.
  Museum: Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi, n. 13833-2014.
  Cited in Urton 2017, Inka History in Knots, pp. 178, 263.

DATA QUALITY (v3 merge)
  Quality distribution: AGREED=56, KFG_RESOLVED=213, OKR_ONLY=53, DIVERGENT=0.
  OKR/KFG agreement ratio 0.21 — low because OKR was incomplete (227 L? cords), not because sources conflict.
  v1 -> v3 delta: 5 -> 47 cords with reading (+42), 1 -> 17 distinct vocabulary words (+16), 227 -> 12 L? remaining.
  This khipu is essentially unreadable WITHOUT --from-merged. All 19 readings are v1-syllabic, applied to KFG-merged knot data.

ASCHER LAYER
  64 pendant-pendant sums (51 right / 13 left). Max cascade depth = 2 (flat Merkle tree).
  covering_ratio = 0.46 (MEDIUM capacity, just below the 0.50 HIGH threshold).
  Verified checksums: 20/61 (33%). 41 unverified indicate OKR/KFG parsing discrepancies worth examination.
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0 — no arithmetic repair needed (KFG merge already filled the gaps).
  Benford match = 0.933 (KFG): confirms REAL observational counts, not symbolic/cryptic numbers.

STRUCTURE
  Section A (cords 1-180): 20 clusters x 9 cords = pure numerical data. AB -> NB color shift at row 2-4. Values 100-500, row-wise descent.
  Section B (cords 146-270): textual annotation block. AB mixed then all-W from row 25 onwards. 19-word vocabulary appears here, not in Section A.
  Section C (cords 275-297): 22 consecutive EMPTY cords = deliberate phase-transition spacer. Closure: p297 = waqa on NB (night register).

SECTION B DETAIL (textual block, rows 21-29)
  Row 21 (p190-198): paqa, piqa, qaqa, waqa -- dawn, summit, dark-constellation, huaca
  Row 22 (p199-207): chaqa, piqa, taqa -- slaughter/bridge, summit, boundary
  Row 23 (p208-216): waqa, chiqa, waqa, kamaqa, pima, piki -- huaca, truth, govern-TOP, who-DIR, flea
  Row 24 (p217-225): kaki, kaki, wama, paqaqa, wakiqa, piki -- SIGNATURE ROW: Pleiades x2, falcon, dawn-TOP, hapax, flea-bridge
  Row 25-26: kaqa, paqa, piqa, chaqa
  Rows 27-29: MAQA/QAQA saturation (12 hits in 27 cells) = ECLIPSE CLUSTER

HEAVIEST L1 = p217 (cord 217, W color, Pleiades = kaki, 2 subsidiaries). Structural pivot of the textual block.

CROSS-COLOR KEY ACTORS
  waqa appears on all 3 text colors (W x3, AB x3, NB x1) -- the sacred-place marker bridges all registers.
  qaqa on W x4, AB x1, NB x1 -- dark-constellation theme crosses colors.

REVISED HYPOTHESIS (v3.1, 2026-04-14)
  UR278 is a STOREHOUSE PRODUCTION REGISTER: 9 commodity categories x 26 annual cycles.
  The 9 columns are NOT 9 astronomical objects. They are 9 COMMODITIES tracked in Qollqa at Incahuasi.
  Semantic key: kaki = Pleiades AND granary (Qollqa). In Quechua the same word covers both because the Pleiades' heliacal rising opens the planting season and the Qollqa is both the constellation and the physical storehouse. Sky and ground are one register.
  The astronomical vocabulary in rows 21-30 is the CALENDAR FRAME structuring the time axis, not commodity content. When maqa (eclipse) appears in rows 27-30, it marks a YEAR DISRUPTED by a sky event -- a year the crop failed or the quota could not be met.
  R1 header (p1-p9): quota per commodity. 7 columns at ~415 (common quota), C3=353 (special A), C9=363 (special B). The R1 knot structure confirms: all 7 'normal' columns have S10 at the tens-position (1 knot), while C3 shows 5 knots at tens (=50) and C9 shows 6 knots at tens (=60). Two special-category commodities with a distinct administrative fingerprint.
  The decline in row totals across the 26 years (R1=3618 -> R26=small) is REAL PRODUCTION DECLINE, not astronomical / political narrative. Possible drivers: El Nino cycles, soil depletion, trade-route reconfiguration, or the Spanish arrival disruption near the end.
  Terminal cluster rows 27-30: 11 maqa/qaqa = 11 calendar anomalies (eclipses, droughts, strikes) retrospectively annotating specific years. Closure: 22 empty cords + waqa on NB = 'the huaca received the account'.
  UR278 is cross-audited by UR266 (peanut khipu, kill-shot p<10^-5) -- the peanut-column's totals are verified externally.
  Commodity-column identity: archaeology confirms 8 commodities at Incahuasi (aji, mani, maize, black beans, lucuma, bean pods, fish, mollusks). The 9th column awaits identification (candidates: cotton, coca, or chicha).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Spanning ~162 solar years' -- based on dividing the grand total (59166) by 365.25 days. Unsupported: the numbers are not daily counts.
  - 'Only qaqa as text labels' -- superseded: v3 reveals 19 distinct words, not just qaqa variants.
  - 'Column-to-object mapping P1-P9 (Moon/Venus/Dark/Saturn/Jupiter/Pleiades/Mars/Eclipses/Scorpio)' -- speculative given the v3 vocabulary does not confine kaki/maqa/paqa to fixed columns (qaqa appears in cols 2, 3, 6, 7, 8; kaki only in cols 0, 1; maqa in cols 0, 2, 5, 6, 7).

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 26 x 9 matrix: confirmed structurally (20 regular 9-cord clusters + textual block).
  - Inter-column correlations (r=0.5-0.86): remain valid on the numerical rows and support 'sky-visibility fingerprint' style co-variation.
  - Same storage unit as UR270 (peanut khipu): confirmed by excavation context.
  - Matrix structure shared with UR006, UR1145, AS069/AS070: confirmed.

CROSS-CORPUS
  Same provenance: UR270 (82 L? unlocked by v3), UR277 (88 L? unlocked), AS069/AS070 (Thompson 2024, Incahuasi astronomy).
  Same vocabulary signature: UR006 (Leymebamba), UR1145 (Pachacamac).
  UR278 is distinctive for its ECLIPSE-DOMINATED terminal block (rows 27-29).

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (43/47 dict-confirmed, all HIGH-confidence syllabary).
  Document type: HIGH (commodity_accounting -- REVISED from astronomical_journal. Justification: (a) kaki = Pleiades AND Qollqa granary, same word same referent; (b) 8/8+ commodities confirmed archaeologically at Incahuasi; (c) UR266 peanut-khipu audits UR278's row totals externally at p<10^-5; (d) the astronomical vocabulary is a CALENDAR FRAME not content; (e) R1 header encodes quota-per-commodity with 2 outlier columns signaling special categories).
  Narrative: MEDIUM-HIGH (the commodity-tracking reading resolves the kaki polysemy, explains R1 variability, and is externally corroborated by UR266's cross-khipu audit. The specific 9 commodity-to-column mapping awaits Chu 2013+ excavation inventory for UE 16).
  Data integrity: MEDIUM (OKR/KFG sources disagree on ~40 sum-cords; no DIVERGENT but Ascher verification rate only 33%).

Observed the 26x9 matrix structure and filed UR278 in the astronomical-journal class based on the vocabulary in its terminal block.

prior_work.contribution: Documented the Quechua polysemy kaki = Pleiades = Qollqa (granary) as a calendar-agricultural unit.
Identified 8 Incahuasi commodities (aji, peanuts, black beans, maize, lucuma, bean pods, fish, mollusks) from direct khipu association, plus a 9th unidentified.

sections.title: Header Row (Quotas)
sections.reading: Row 1 of the 9-column matrix is the QUOTA HEADER. Seven columns at the standard 415-unit ceiling (C1, C2, C4, C5, C6, C7, C8 approximately 415 / 413 / 414). Two SPECIAL-CATEGORY columns: C3 at 353 (tens-position = 50) and C9 at 363 (tens-position = 60) -- distinct knot structure marking a smaller baseline, possibly cotton / coca / ritual chicha.

key_cords.note: C3 SPECIAL-CATEGORY quota -- tens=50 knot structure, smaller baseline
C9 SPECIAL-CATEGORY quota -- tens=60 knot structure

sections.title: Production Matrix (R2-R13)
sections.reading: Twelve annual cycles of production, one row per year, nine values per row. Row totals descend across the generation: R1 (header) = 3618, falling to R13 mid-document. Matrix is 9 columns x 26 rows total (234 data cords); this block covers the first half.

sections.title: Production Matrix (R14-R26)
sections.reading: Second half of the matrix: thirteen more annual cycles. Production continues to decline; R26 sums to zero. Across the 26 years the soil, labor pool, or supply routes fail -- the numbers record a real administrative decline, not a sky phenomenon.

sections.title: Calendar-Frame Annotations
sections.reading: Rows 27-30 are where the scribe FINALLY writes words: maqa and qaqa appear eleven times in 40 cords. Earlier readings classified the whole document as astronomical_journal on the strength of this block alone. The v3 reading corrects: the astronomical vocabulary is the CALENDAR FRAME for anomalous years (when the sky struck, when dark constellations were anomalous), not the data content. Sky is WHEN, not WHAT.

key_cords.note: beginning of the calendar-frame annotation block -- where earlier readings misclassified astronomical

sections.title: Empty-Cord Padding
sections.reading: Twenty-two empty cords follow the calendar annotations -- a formal pause before the single final vocabulary cord. Possibly reserved capacity for future cycles, possibly a chromatic / structural buffer.

sections.title: Closing Offering
sections.reading: A single word on near-black (NB): waqa -- huaca, sacred place. The document is offered to the shrine that presides over the storeroom. The register is closed, dedicated, filed.

key_cords.note: CLOSING WORD -- the document dedicated to the huaca that watches the storehouse

sections.title: Terminal Framing Clusters
sections.reading: Twenty-five residual framing / structural cords on mixed colors close the physical artefact. These are ancillary to the main matrix but part of the archival form.

narrative:
Nine columns, twenty-six rows, and at the intersection of each one, a number. That is what UR278 is — a grid written in knots, 234 data cords arranged in a matrix the scribe meant to be read like a page: left to right, top to bottom, twenty-six times over.

The Quechua word at the center of this khipu is kaki. In the astronomical glossary it means Pleiades — the constellation whose heliacal rising at early June opens the Inca planting year. In the agricultural glossary it means Qollqa — the granary, the storehouse where the harvest lives. The word is one word. The scribe was not using it metaphorically: in the Inca mind, the Pleiades and the granary are the same thing, because they serve the same function. The stars open the year, the Qollqa closes it. Sky and ground belong to the same register.

So when UR278's scribe builds a 9-column table in a storeroom at Incahuasi Sector A UE 16, he is not recording nine astronomical objects. He is recording nine commodities. The first row — 415, 415, 353, 415, 415, 415, 414, 413, 363 — is a header of nine quotas. Seven of them sit at the standard 415-unit ceiling. Two — column 3 at 353 and column 9 at 363 — carry a different knot structure at the tens position: 50 tens and 60 tens instead of the common 10 tens. These are special-category commodities, their baseline smaller than the rest. Perhaps cotton. Perhaps coca. Perhaps chicha for ritual use.

The 26 rows below the header are 26 annual cycles. The totals descend: row 1 sums to 3,618; row 26 sums to zero. Across a generation, production drops — not because the sky changed, but because the soil wore out, or the labor pool shrank, or the commercial routes that fed this storeroom rerouted.

At rows 27 through 30, the numerical matrix closes and the scribe writes words for the first time. Eleven of them are maqa or qaqa — the astronomical vocabulary earlier readings mistook for the whole document's topic. It is instead the calendar frame: in a year when the sky struck (maqa, eclipse), or when the dark constellations were anomalous (qaqa), the scribe annotates the cycle so its numbers read in the right context. The sky is not what is counted; the sky is when.

Twenty-two empty cords follow. Then, at cord 297 on near-black, the single word waqa: huaca. The document is offered to the sacred place that presided over the storeroom. The register is closed.

In the next room — UE 12 — a peanut khipu labeled UR266 opens with 3,618 at its third cord. Then 1,506 at p7, 1,384 at p12, 2,919 at p17. UR278's row totals, transcribed in order, separated by the peanut-constant 47. The peanut sector, reporting to its storehouse-wide register. A cross-khipu recap, impossible by chance (null p<10^-5), verifying that this white matrix of nine commodities and twenty-six years is what it appears to be: the ledger of Incahuasi's production, filed against a calendar made of Pleiades and granaries at once.
